title: Architecture: server composition
task: How does FastMCP's server mounting/composition system work? Explain how you can compose multiple MCP servers together and how requests are routed between them. Do NOT make any code changes.
output:
Now I have a clear picture. Here's how FastMCP's server composition system works:

---

## FastMCP Server Composition

### The Two Approaches (and one deprecated)

**`mount()`** â€” the primary API â€” creates a **live, dynamic connection** between servers. The mounted server's components are queried at request time, so changes to the mounted server are immediately reflected. Lifespans and middleware of the mounted server are both honored.

**`import_server()`** is deprecated in favor of `mount()`. It was a one-time, static copy: at call time it iterated the source server's tools/resources/prompts and registered clones into the importing server. No lifespan, no middleware propagation.

---

### The Provider Architecture

Internally, `mount()` creates a **`FastMCPProvider`** wrapping the child server and appends it to the parent's `_providers` list (`server.py:2640-2645`).

Each `FastMCP` server maintains:
- Its own local `_tool_manager`, `_resource_manager`, `_prompt_manager` (statically registered components)
- A `_providers: list[Provider]` â€” the dynamic composition layer

The `Provider` base class (`providers/base.py`) defines a uniform interface: `list_tools()`, `get_tool()`, `list_resources()`, `get_resource()`, `list_resource_templates()`, `get_resource_template()`, `list_prompts()`, `get_prompt()`, plus execution methods (`call_tool()`, `read_resource()`, `render_prompt()`).

---

### Request Routing

**Listing** (`get_tools()`, `get_resources()`, `get_prompts()`): The parent server merges local components with all providers. **Local components always win** over provider components for name conflicts (`server.py:1005-1007`). Among providers, later-registered ones override earlier ones.

**Lookup/execution** (`get_tool(key)`, etc.): Local managers are checked first; on `NotFoundError`, providers are queried in registration order. The **first provider returning non-`None` wins** (`server.py:698-715`). This is a linear scan â€” no indexing.

---

### Middleware Delegation via Wrapper Components

`FastMCPProvider` doesn't just proxy names. It wraps each child component in a specialized class:

- `FastMCPProviderTool` â€” when `.run()` is called, it calls `child_server._call_tool_middleware(original_name, args)` (`fastmcp_provider.py:105`)
- `FastMCPProviderResource` â€” delegates to `child_server._read_resource_middleware(original_uri)`
- `FastMCPProviderPrompt` â€” delegates to `child_server._get_prompt_content_middleware(original_name, args)`
- `FastMCPProviderResourceTemplate` â€” creates `FastMCPProviderResource` instances on demand

This means **the child server's entire middleware chain runs** when one of its components is invoked through the parent.

---

### Namespacing via `TransformingProvider`

When `mount(server, namespace="sub")` is called, a `TransformingProvider` wraps the `FastMCPProvider` (`server.py:2641-2644`):

| Component | Transformation |
|-----------|----------------|
| Tools | `tool_name` â†’ `sub_tool_name` |
| Prompts | `prompt_name` â†’ `sub_prompt_name` |
| Resources | `proto://path` â†’ `proto://sub/path` |
| Templates | `proto://path/{id}` â†’ `proto://sub/path/{id}` |

`TransformingProvider` reverses the transformation on lookup: a request for `sub_tool_name` strips the prefix to get `tool_name` before querying the inner provider (`transforming.py:179-187`). Tool renames (`tool_names={"verbose": "short"}`) bypass namespacing entirely.

Without a namespace, components are exposed with their original names. Multiple un-namespaced servers are tried in registration order.

---

### Lifespan

`FastMCPProvider.lifespan()` runs the child server's user-defined `_lifespan` when the parent starts up (`fastmcp_provider.py:615`). It does **not** run the child's full `_lifespan_manager()` (which would spin up a separate Docket instance); the parent server's Docket handles background tasks for the whole composition tree.

---

### Custom Providers

Beyond `FastMCPProvider`, you can implement `Provider` directly to serve tools/resources/prompts from any dynamic source (a database, an external API, etc.) and register it via `server.add_provider(provider)` or the `providers=` constructor argument.

Now I have a clear picture. Here's how FastMCP's server composition system works:

---

## FastMCP Server Composition

### The Two Approaches (and one deprecated)

**`mount()`** â€” the primary API â€” creates a **live, dynamic connection** between servers. The mounted server's components are queried at request time, so changes to the mounted server are immediately reflected. Lifespans and middleware of the mounted server are both honored.

**`import_server()`** is deprecated in favor of `mount()`. It was a one-time, static copy: at call time it iterated the source server's tools/resources/prompts and registered clones into the importing server. No lifespan, no middleware propagation.

---

### The Provider Architecture

Internally, `mount()` creates a **`FastMCPProvider`** wrapping the child server and appends it to the parent's `_providers` list (`server.py:2640-2645`).

Each `FastMCP` server maintains:
- Its own local `_tool_manager`, `_resource_manager`, `_prompt_manager` (statically registered components)

The `Provider` base class (`providers/base.py`) defines a uniform interface: `list_tools()`, `get_tool()`, `list_resources()`, `get_resource()`, `list_resource_templates()`, `get_resource_template()`, `list_prompts()`, `get_prompt()`, plus execution methods (`call_tool()`, `read_resource()`, `render_prompt()`).

---

### Request Routing

**Listing** (`get_tools()`, `get_resources()`, `get_prompts()`): The parent server merges local components with all providers. **Local components always win** over provider components for name conflicts (`server.py:1005-1007`). Among providers, later-registered ones override earlier ones.

**Lookup/execution** (`get_tool(key)`, etc.): Local managers are checked first; on `NotFoundError`, providers are queried in registration order. The **first provider returning non-`None` wins** (`server.py:698-715`). This is a linear scan â€” no indexing.

---

### Middleware Delegation via Wrapper Components

`FastMCPProvider` doesn't just proxy names. It wraps each child component in a specialized class:

- `FastMCPProviderTool` â€” when `.run()` is called, it calls `child_server._call_tool_middleware(original_name, args)` (`fastmcp_provider.py:105`)
- `FastMCPProviderResource` â€” delegates to `child_server._read_resource_middleware(original_uri)`
- `FastMCPProviderPrompt` â€” delegates to `child_server._get_prompt_content_middleware(original_name, args)`
- `FastMCPProviderResourceTemplate` â€” creates `FastMCPProviderResource` instances on demand

This means **the child server's entire middleware chain runs** when one of its components is invoked through the parent.

---

When `mount(server, namespace="sub")` is called, a `TransformingProvider` wraps the `FastMCPProvider` (`server.py:2641-2644`):

| Component | Transformation |
|-----------|----------------|
| Tools | `tool_name` â†’ `sub_tool_name` |
| Prompts | `prompt_name` â†’ `sub_prompt_name` |
| Resources | `proto://path` â†’ `proto://sub/path` |
| Templates | `proto://path/{id}` â†’ `proto://sub/path/{id}` |

Without a namespace, components are exposed with their original names. Multiple un-namespaced servers are tried in registration order.

---

### Lifespan

`FastMCPProvider.lifespan()` runs the child server's user-defined `_lifespan` when the parent starts up (`fastmcp_provider.py:615`). It does **not** run the child's full `_lifespan_manager()` (which would spin up a separate Docket instance); the parent server's Docket handles background tasks for the whole composition tree.

---

### Custom Providers

Beyond `FastMCPProvider`, you can implement `Provider` directly to serve tools/resources/prompts from any dynamic source (a database, an external API, etc.) and register it via `server.add_provider(provider)` or the `providers=` constructor argument.
